event: Portrait of Saint Luke the Evangelist with his symbol the ox
symbols: ox/bull — the traditional symbol of Luke's Gospel, emphasizing sacrifice and priestly service
upward gaze — inspiration and divine illumination for writing the Gospel
close proximity of man and animal — the unity of the evangelist with his inspired symbol
circular tondo format — completeness and eternity, fitting for the eternal Gospel
description: A circular tondo painting rendered in deep warm browns and earth tones showing a half-length portrait of a man with a strong, youthful face gazing upward and to the right. Beside him, partially visible, is the head of a light-colored bull or ox. The man wears a reddish-brown robe. The painting is very dark with dramatic chiaroscuro, light falling primarily on the man's face and the animal's head. The brushwork is bold and expressive, characteristic of Titian's late manner.
theological_description: Saint Luke the Evangelist is shown with his traditional symbol, the ox or bull, one of the four living creatures of Ezekiel 1:10 and Revelation 4:7 that were assigned to the four evangelists by early Church Fathers. The ox was associated with Luke because his Gospel begins with Zechariah's priestly service in the Temple (Luke 1:5-25), and the ox is a sacrificial animal — Luke's Gospel uniquely emphasizes Christ's sacrificial mission and compassion for the marginalized. Luke was also Paul's 'beloved physician' (Colossians 4:14) and companion in missionary journeys, and tradition credits him with writing both the third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles. His upward gaze in this portrait suggests the divine inspiration that guided his careful research — Luke uniquely claims to have 'followed all things closely for some time past, to write an orderly account' (Luke 1:3). The four evangelist symbols derive from the heavenly worship scene of Revelation 4:6-8, where the four living creatures ceaselessly praise God, and from Ezekiel's throne vision (Ezekiel 1:4-14), linking the earthly Gospels to the heavenly reality they reveal.

scripture_refs.reason: Luke's prologue declaring his careful investigation and orderly account — the evangelist's own testimony
The four living creatures around the throne — the source of the evangelist symbol of the ox
Ezekiel's vision of the four living creatures including the ox — the Old Testament root of evangelist symbolism
Paul identifies Luke as the beloved physician — establishing Luke's historical identity
Luke's Gospel opening with Zechariah's priestly Temple service — the reason the sacrificial ox was assigned to Luke
